item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: honestly just bad. the clerical tone is nonexistent, it feels like reading an 8th graders fan fiction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason I feel that scp-096 would be the best thing to cross test scp-021 with then realized that would just make a forever angry 096.

item.title: Re: This needs something
item.content:encoded: where can we find this Euclid you seek

item.title: This needs something
item.content:encoded: something called Euclid

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cassie's pet dragon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article also mentions various benefits the SCP gives to it´s host, ironically including resistance to pain during stressing situations

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The symbiotic relationship is usually limited by how long the host can tolerate such pain in everyday life. This has culminated in suicide in a number of subjects. In rare cases, hosts have also fallen victim to fatal skin infections.

I know I'm probably nitpicking here, but as described the SCP does not have a symbiotic relationship with its host. Symbiosis is defined as both involved parties receiving benefit from one another, here SCP-021 is simply a parasite as stated earlier in the article since its host receives only pain and loss of skin pigment. As I said, probably nitpicking, but just something that stood out to me. Overall a great article with an excellent idea and magnificent execution, a +1 from me for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holds up surprisingly well, but still suffers from tonal issues, and is overall uninteresting. Downvoting, but I would love to see a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An old one, but one of the best in my opinion.
+1

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded:
The 1st image in this article is a copyrighted image from http://www.andyshou.com/ and is used here with the permission of the copyright holder.


This automated message was sent on behalf of anqxyr. Please do not reply to this message or send other messages to this bot. Contact anqxyr in regard to this message. If you believe that you are not the intended recipient of this message, that the image was delivered incorrectly, or have other concerns about this bot, please contact anqxyr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since the last image was deleted, maybe this could be a substitute?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One or several images in this article have been removed due to non-compliance with the Creative Commons license. They may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact anqxyr or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.


This automated message was sent on behalf of anqxyr. Please do not reply to this message or send other messages to this bot. Contact anqxyr in regard to this message. If you believe that you are not the intended recipient of this message, that the image was delivered incorrectly, or have other concerns about this bot, please contact anqxyr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It would make a pretty sick looking tattoo if it wern't for the excruciating pain part.

That being said, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yay, I made it turn to +200!

This holds up really well for such an old article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sexual intercourse appears to be the most reliable method of transfer, with a 93% rate of transmission.

Transmission between species is unknown; previous tests suggest it to be either impossible or exceedingly rare.

Upvoted for the implication that the Foundation has tried having D-Class engage in bestiality as an experimental procedure. I'm no stranger to them doing questionable things to maintain containment, but that one is both new and different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These are my favourite kind of SCPs. No world-shattering secrets, no reality-breaking powers; just a silly little tattoo that eats other tattoos and causes pain to its host.

+1

(Re: origins, it's possible that it isn't man-made; this could be a "some person was inspired by the crazy dragon thing they found in nature and decided to start trying to make their own." Alternatively, if it /was/ artificial, it could have been made by someone so masterful that they were able to create a tattoo of the same quality as the ones we see today. I mean, it /is/ a living tattoo, after all. That requires a pretty high degree of skill on its own.

item.title: Loved it!
item.content:encoded: Almost makes you want one eh?

item.title: What about bruises?
item.content:encoded: I wonder how and or if 21 would interact with bruises on the hosts skin. Also what about skin-cancer? I like AmpMunkeys idea and this would be a great way to establish wether or not 21 makes its host aggressive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sexual intercourse appears to be the most reliable method of transfer, with a 93% rate of transmission. However, due to the severe pain involved, this is less than ideal.

It was almost definitely a smaller amount of tests, but I want to imagine that they tried it 100 times… Damn!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I very much like this.

It is a simple and clear concept, expressed with economy.

Fascinating. Easy to visualize with a well-chosen photo. Great work.
It's about time this one hit 100.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one. The only issue I have is the line that the hosts death doesn't seem to affect that organism. If the tattoo feeds on melanin, wouldn't it run out pretty quick when the host dies? I know it could be transferred from dead to living, but I'd think the death of the host would eventually starve the tattoo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if SCP-021 were transferred to a host without a pain sensation? If the host feels no pain then there won't be an issue right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this one. Very unique idea and writing style. Up-voted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Possibly because 021 might attempt to eat it before it is finished. Which might be a problem or it might not. Maybe if the food isn't finished, lines that have not been completed (gone in a full circle. Think of a half-completed apple or orange. It would form a sort of semi-circle which would have two ends of the line) are "sharp" in a 2-D world and would pierce 021, potentially seriously wounding and/or killing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice, unique entry. The only problem I have is why must tattoos for the purposes of feeding be applied quickly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the terrible choice present for a D-class host: You get to avoid monthly termination, but you're locked up with an excruciatingly painful tattoo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh, up-voted if only for the creepy subtext in 'Transfer is more complicated in deceased subjects'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no canon, so whatever time period works for you. Most ping it around the turn of the century, or perhaps during WW2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Assuming that this SCP does not change it's appearance and that it was created by a human, the artistic style and ink colors used would give an estimate of it's origin date. I am no expert on tattoos, but the one in the photo seems more modern than the earliest days of the Foundation. Which brings up a question I have. When was the Foundation founded? I have seen references to SCPs being first noted in 18XX.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also keep in mind that in-universe, we don't know when this SCP was catalogued or last updated. It could have been decades ago. Or the research team might not have clearance to know about SCP-085. Of course, either way, there is no canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Each SCP is to be taken on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this could use a bit of editing, mainly because it claims that we don't know anything about possible intelligence or sentience in two-dimensional beings, which seems to be in direct opposition to SCP-085.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've always loved this entry.

item.title: Ooh
item.content:encoded: Fascinating. I love it.
